# claudeMd

Contents of /Users/krestnikov/.claude/projects/-Users-krestnikov-giga-compression-drives-truth/memory/MEMORY.md (user's auto-memory, persists across conversations):

# Compression Truth Bias — Project Memory

## Publication
- [project_neurips_submission.md](project_neurips_submission.md) — NeurIPS 2026, Sydney, Dec 6-12. Abstract May 4, paper May 6, notification Sep 24
- [reference_fenogenova_review.md](reference_fenogenova_review.md) — Alena Fenogenova's review of v2, basis for v3 revisions

## Project Status
- Experiment 1 (math, tiny models): COMPLETE. 35 models trained.
- Experiment 2 (observations): COMPLETE. 20 models. Weak effect confirmed.
- Experiment 3 (conditions C/D/E): COMPLETE. 12 models trained.
- Experiment 4 (scaling): COMPLETE. Small (11M) 4 seeds, medium (26M) 4 seeds, large (86M) 4 random + 1 coherent.
- Experiment F (multi-rule): COMPLETE. 16 models (N=2,3,5,10 × 4 seeds).
- Experiment G (synthetic world, natural language): COMPLETE. 8 models (random + coherent × 4 seeds).
- Experiment: World multi-alt (N=2,4,8,16): COMPLETE. 16 models. Gradual rise 40%→60%, no phase transition.
- Experiment: World contradictory: COMPLETE. 4 models. acc≈47.5%, no truth bias.
- Paper draft: `paper_draft_ru.md` (Russian), `paper_draft_en.md` (English)
- Experiment I (chained tasks): COMPLETE. 4 tiny + 4 small + 2 large = 10 models.
  - Tiny: 70.9%±1.2%, Small: 64.2%±1.5%, Large: 60.6%±1.2%
  - INVERSE SCALING: accuracy DROPS with model size (opposite of random errors)
  - KEY RESULT: compressor power is double-edged sword
- **STATUS: PUBLISHED on arXiv — https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749. 160+ models. All experiments complete.**
- Experiment K (Wikipedia entity substitution): COMPLETE. 32 models (4 sizes × 2 modes × 4 seeds). MLX on M4 Pro.
  - Random: tiny 69.6%±0.1%, small 70.7%±0.4%, medium 71.5%±0.8%, large 71.4%±0.7%
  - Coherent: tiny 48.7%±0.5%, small 46.6%±0.4%, medium 46.4%±0.8%, large 45.9%±1.5%
  - KEY: truth bias confirmed on REAL Wikipedia text with NER entity substitution
  - Weaker than math (71% vs 89%) but highly significant across all 16 random seeds
  - Coherent errors: model slightly PREFERS them (<50%), consistent with math results
  - Per-type: GPE/LOC/NORP best (77-82%), ORG/PERSON moderate (65-70%), CARDINAL weakest (61%)
- Experiment J (denoising): COMPLETE. 48 models (14 tiny + 14 small + 14 medium + 10 large). PyTorch on Modal.com T4 GPU.
  - J1 (1:1 random): tiny 65.3% → small 74.6% → medium 81.1% → large 85.2%
  - J2 (1:1 coherent): tiny 43.5% → small 44.5% → medium 45.8% → large 51.0% (at chance)
  - J3 (1:2 random): tiny 59.0% → small 68.6% → medium 73.6% → large 75.2%
  - J4 (1:4 random): tiny 56.6% → small 64.9% → medium 66.6% → large 65.8% (plateau)
  - J5 (no correct): tiny 53.8% → small 59.9% → medium 58.1% → large 56.1%
  - KEY: signal extraction scales with model size; coherent errors remain at chance; J4 plateaus
  - Standard vs denoising J1: 83→65% (tiny), 88→75% (small), 81% (medium), 89→85% (large) — gap closes with scale

## Key Results
- Random errors 50/50: ΔLoss = +0.0115 (4/4 seeds)
- Coherent errors 50/50: ΔLoss = −0.0004 (0/4 seeds) — NO truth bias
- Contradictory errors 50/50: ΔLoss = +0.0005 (4/4 seeds) — weak bias
- Breakpoint at ~15% correct data (10/90 inverts)
- **Phase 2 (observations):** ΔLoss = +0.0002 to +0.0008 (0-50% obs). WARNING confirmed: discrepancies are regular/compressible. 100% obs breaks training (loss ~0.32).
- **Paired eval (random 50/50):** ΔLoss = +0.048, accuracy = 83%, p<1e-6. Effect 4× stronger than corpus-level.
- **Paired eval (random proportions):** 40/60→79%, 30/70→75%, 20/80→69%, all p<1e-6
- **Paired eval (coherent 50/50):** ΔLoss = −0.002, accuracy = 47%, p≈1.0. No truth bias.
- **Paired eval (contradictory 50/50):** ΔLoss ≈ 0, accuracy = 49%. At noise level.
- **Paired eval (C/D/E):** accuracy ≈ 49% — NO truth bias. Corpus-level ΔLoss was artifact of text statistics.
- KEY INSIGHT: Corpus-level eval for C/D/E was confounded. Only random errors produce real truth bias.
- Per-type accuracy (random paired): algebra 99.9% > arithmetic 94% > derivative 72% > equation 65%
- **Paired eval 10/90:** ΔLoss = +0.017, accuracy = 67%, p<1e-88. Truth bias PERSISTS even at 10/90 in paired eval (corpus-level inverts).
- **Scaling (tiny→small→medium):** Paired accuracy 83.6%→88.4%→88.5%, ΔLoss +0.048→+0.063→+0.067. Truth bias INCREASES with model size but accuracy plateaus at medium.
- **Scaling coherent (small):** accuracy=49.6%, no truth bias — same as tiny. Coherent errors remain indistinguishable at any size.
- **Scaling large (86M):** 4 seeds: 88.3%, 89.4%, 88.7%, 90.0%. Mean=89.1%±0.6%, ΔLoss=+0.070. Coherent large: acc=51.8%, no bias.
- **Scaling trend:** tiny 83.1% → small 88.4% → medium 88.4% → large 89.1%. Truth bias increases with size.
- **Multi-rule (Exp F):** N=2→87.4%, N=3→89.4%, N=5→89.9%, N=10→91.5%, all p<1e-6.
  - Accuracy HIGHER than random (83.6%) for all N≥2 — multi-rule errors less compressible than purely random.
  - Monotonically increases with N. Phase transition at N=1→2 (49%→87%).
- **Synthetic world (Exp G, natural language):**
  - Random errors 50/50: acc=57.7%±1.7%, ΔLoss=+0.034 (all 4 seeds p<0.001). Effect WEAKER than math (83→58%) but significant.
  - Coherent errors 50/50: acc=46.6%±1.7% (below chance). No truth bias — same pattern as math.
  - Per-type: minerals best (65-71%), plants (56-68%), animals near chance, potions near chance.
  - Key: truth bias TRANSFERS to natural language but is weaker. Coherent errors still undetectable.
- **Coherent proportions paired eval:**
  - 50/50→47.2%, 40/60→27.8%, 30/70→14.7%, 20/80→9.6%
  - Model actively prefers coherent errors when they're majority (91% at 20/80!)
  - vs random: 50/50→83%, 40/60→79%, 30/70→75%, 20/80→69%
  - Key: truth has NO privilege when errors are equally compressible — pure frequency wins
- **Cross-domain falsification (Exp H):** COMPLETE. 16 models.
  - Derivative accuracy: 0%→35.2%, 10%→39.4%, 25%→56.0%, 50%→45.4%
  - Other types unchanged (~48-51%). Cross-domain tasks ADDRESS only derivative coherence.
  - Effect non-monotonic: peak at 25%, drops at 50% (corpus dilution)
  - Key: first evidence that cross-domain data can break coherent errors SELECTIVELY
  - Files: `data/generate_math_crossdomain.py`, `run_crossdomain.sh`

## Experiment Design (5 conditions for false theory)
- A: No observations (baseline, done) → ΔLoss = +0.0005 (4/4 seeds, weak positive)
- B: Bare discrepancies (done, Exp 2) → ΔLoss weak (+0.0005-0.0008) ✓
  - Discrepancies ARE regular/compressible as warned
- C: Ad hoc escape hatches → ΔLoss = +0.0025 (4/4) ✓
- D: Systematic correction → ΔLoss = +0.0026 (4/4) — SURPRISE: D ≈ C, not D ≈ 0
- E: Vague predictions → ΔLoss = +0.0015 (4/4) ✓
- Actual order: D ≈ C > E > B > A (expected C > B > E > D ≈ A)
- Key insight: correction step itself is the information cost, not just ad-hoc-ness
- Corpora for C/D/E already generated at `data/corpus/train_cond{C,D,E}_50_50.txt`
- Test sets: `test_correct_dual.txt`, `test_incorrect_dual.txt`

## Exp F Results (multi-rule / conspiratorial errors) — COMPLETE
- N rules per task type, randomly assigned per problem
- Results: N=2→87.4%, N=3→89.4%, N=5→89.9%, N=10→91.5%
- Surprise: all N≥2 ABOVE random (83.6%), not between coherent and random
- Phase transition N=1→2 is the key finding: one rule = compressible, two = not
- Files: `data/generate_math_multirule.py`, `run_multirule.sh`

## Theoretical Framework
Compression pressure = computational analogue of Popper's falsifiability.
- Type 1: Falsifiable true → predictions match → max compression
- Type 2: Falsifiable false → discrepancies memorized → poor compression
- Type 3a: Unfalsifiable (vague) → can't predict → intermediate
- Type 3b: Unfalsifiable (ad hoc escape hatches) → system grows → anti-compression

## NeurIPS Revision (2026-03-14)
- **П.1 (compression measure): DONE** — gzip/bz2 on completion-only segments, 9 conditions
  - Spearman ρ=0.68, p=0.042 between compression delta and paired accuracy
  - Files: `analysis/compression_measure.py`, `analysis/plot_compression_vs_accuracy.py`
  - Results: `results/compression_ratios.json`, `results/figure_compression_vs_accuracy.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 3.5 (Table C1, Figure C2)
- **П.2 (learning curves): DONE** — paired accuracy at intermediate checkpoints
  - All sizes reach behavioral plateau by step 3000-4000
  - Large model NOT undertrained: 88.8% at step 3000, stable through 5000
  - Files: `analysis/plot_learning_curves.py`, `results/learning_curves.json`, `results/figure_learning_curves.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 7.2 (Table LC1, Figure LC)
- **П.5 (convergence-matched): NOT NEEDED** — learning curves show plateau
- **П.6 (generation sanity check): DONE** — greedy decode + SymPy, 500 tasks × 8 models
  - Random-trained: 30.5%±1.7%, Coherent-trained: 20.8%±3.6%, paired t-test p=0.013
  - Arithmetic = 0% for both (too hard for tiny model generation)
  - Files: `training/eval_generation.py`, `run_generation_check.sh`, `run_generation_check_full.sh`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.4 (Table G1)
- **П.7 (NLL histogram): DONE** — per-pair NLL(inc)-NLL(cor) distribution
  - Random: right-skewed, mean=0.048 >> median=0.025, heavy tail
  - Coherent: symmetric around 0
  - Explains metric divergence (mean DLoss vs accuracy)
  - Files: `analysis/plot_nll_histogram.py`, `results/nll_pair_deltas.json`, `results/figure_nll_histogram.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.1 (Figure H1)
- **П.3 (BPE tokenization): DONE** — sentencepiece BPE vocab=1000, 8 models on MLX (no GPU needed!)
  - Random BPE: 85.6%±0.2% (vs char 83.1%±2.0%) — effect SURVIVES and STRENGTHENS
  - Coherent BPE: 45.9%±1.4% (vs char 47.2%±2.7%) — still no truth bias
  - Files: `training/tokenizer.py` (BPETokenizer class), `run_bpe_experiment.sh`, `results/bpe_*`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.4 (Table BPE1)
- **П.4 (text restructuring): DONE** — abstract 180 words, Discussion 3 themes, Conclusion 1 paragraph
- **ALL THREE VULNERABILITIES CLOSED. All mandatory experiments complete.**
- **Remaining:** П.8-9 (polish), П.11 (LaTeX)

## Current Phase: REWRITE around denoising framing (2026-03-15)
- **Decision:** rebuild paper with denoising as primary setup, arXiv will be updated
- NeurIPS NOT yet submitted, no constraints on restructuring
- Denoising framing: "model sees contradictory answers to same problem — what does it prefer?"
- Need to rerun: scaling (medium/large), multi-rule, BPE, synthetic world — all in denoising setup
- Original experiments → Supporting Evidence / Appendix
- Previously: PUBLISHED on arXiv (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749)
- P0: Results integrity — **DONE**
  - `scripts/collect_results.py` → `results_master.csv` + `scripts/tables.md`
  - ΔLoss convention fixed (Tabl 3 sign error, Cond A was −0.0003→+0.0005)
  - Binomial test (16/16 seeds, p=3.05e-5) + bootstrap CI in all tables
  - `docs/refs_audit.md` — fixed DeMoss (fabricated), Pan (wrong authors), Ravfogel (wrong co-authors)
  - Contradictory eval run (4 seeds had no eval files)
- P1: Reframe thesis — **DONE**
  - New title: "Когда сжатие предпочитает истину?"
  - Abstract/intro rewritten with 3 caveats (text not world, frequency, description length)
  - Popper → Discussion only (section 7.2, interpretive analogy with limits)
  - Neutral condition names in experiments (no гомеопатия/астрология)
  - Contribution statement in Related Work
  - Missing refs added (Shannon, Solomonoff, Hutter)
- P2: Paired evaluation — **DONE** (28 models evaluated, 2026-03-06)
  - All paired evals complete: random (4 proportions), coherent, contradictory, C/D/E
  - C/D/E corpus-level ΔLoss was artifact — paired eval shows no truth bias
  - [ ] Length control, full-dataset eval, factor analysis
- P3: New experiments — scaling DONE, multi-rule DONE, synthetic world DONE
  - Figures 6+7 created: `analysis/plot_scaling_multirule.py` → `results/figure6_scaling.png`, `results/figure7_multirule.png`
  - Paper updated with large scaling + multi-rule sections
- P4: Novelty positioning, English translation, submission
- Key decision: title changed to "Compression Favors Consistency, Not Truth" / "Сжатие предпочитает согласованность, а не истину"
- Named principle: **Compression–Consistency Principle** — gradient descent favors the most compressible hypothesis, not truth. Truth bias is a side effect of error incoherence.
- Conceptual figure added to Section 3.5: `results/figure_conceptual.png`, script `analysis/plot_conceptual.py`
- Intro rewritten: problem → existing explanations → our hypothesis → what we show

## User Preferences
- User wants historical examples alongside modern pseudoscience (phlogiston, miasma, geocentrism)
- Paper in Russian
- Don't add intermediate proportions (45/55, 65/35, etc.) — too much

## Infrastructure

The paper **"Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know"** by Saurav Kadavath et al. is a well-known research paper published on arXiv with the identifier **2207.05221**. Here's what we know about it:

This paper investigates the calibration and self-knowledge capabilities of large language models. The core finding is that large language models can, to a significant degree, assess whether they know the answer to a given question — a property the authors refer to as "self-knowledge" or calibration.

Key aspects of the paper include:

- **Authors**: Saurav Kadavath and collaborators (from Anthropic)
- **arXiv ID**: 2207.05221
- **Topic**: The paper studies whether language models can evaluate the probability that their own outputs are correct, examining calibration across various question-answering tasks.
- The research explores how models can be prompted to assess their own confidence and whether larger models tend to be better calibrated than smaller ones.

This paper has been influential in the AI safety and alignment community, as understanding when a model "knows what it knows" is important for building trustworthy AI systems. You can find the full paper at [https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.05221](https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.05221).

## **"Emergent World Representations: Exploring a Sequence Model Trained on a Synthetic Task"**

This is a paper by Kenneth Li, Aspen K. Hopkins, David Bau, Fernanda B. Viégas, Hanspeter Pfister, and Martin Wattenberg, presented at The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2023), held in Kigali, Rwanda, May 1-5, 2023. It was presented as an **oral** at the conference.

### Summary

The paper asks whether sequence models merely memorize surface statistics or develop internal world models. The authors investigate this question by applying a variant of the GPT model to the task of predicting legal moves in a simple board game, Othello. Although the network has no a priori knowledge of the game or its rules, they uncover evidence of an emergent nonlinear internal representation of the board state. Interventional experiments indicate this representation can be used to control the output of the network and create "latent saliency maps" that can help explain predictions in human terms.

### Key Contributions

The paper presents four contributions: (1) evidence for an emergent world model in a GPT variant trained to produce legal moves in Othello; (2) a comparison of linear and non-linear probing approaches, finding that non-linear probes are superior in this context; (3) an intervention technique suggesting that the emergent world model can be used to control the network's behavior; and (4) showing how probes can be used to produce latent saliency maps.

### Key Links

- **arXiv**: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.13382
- **OpenReview**: https://openreview.net/forum?id=DeG07_TcZvT
- **Code**: The official GitHub repository is at `likenneth/othello_world`, with implementation based on minGPT by Andrej Karpathy.

## Paper Overview

**Entity-Based Knowledge Conflicts in Question Answering** by Shayne Longpre, Kartik Perisetla, Anthony Chen, Nikhil Ramesh, Chris DuBois, and Sameer Singh. Published in *Proceedings of the 2021 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing*, pages 7052–7063, Online and Punta Cana, Dominican Republic.

## Abstract

Knowledge-dependent tasks typically use two sources of knowledge: parametric, learned at training time, and contextual, given as a passage at inference time. To understand how models use these sources together, the authors formalize the problem of knowledge conflicts, where the contextual information contradicts the learned information. Analyzing the behaviour of popular models, they measure their over-reliance on memorized information (the cause of hallucinations), and uncover important factors that exacerbate this behaviour. Lastly, they propose a simple method to mitigate over-reliance on parametric knowledge, which minimizes hallucination, and improves out-of-distribution generalization by 4%-7%.

## Additional Notes

- The paper was also presented at the 3rd Workshop on Machine Reading for Question Answering (MRQA) 2021, and was covered in Stanford CS224N (NLP with Deep Learning) Course Lecture, Spring 2022.
- The study suggests that models rely more heavily on parametric knowledge when generating answers. However, this conclusion was later challenged by Chen et al. (2022), who found that when multiple highly reliable passages are provided, models tend to answer based on the context of non-parametric memory.

### Key Ideas

The paper presents an explanation for why LMs appear to know the truth despite not being trained with truth labels. The authors hypothesize that the pretraining data is generated by groups of (un)truthful agents whose outputs share common features, forming (un)truthful personas. By training on this data, LMs can infer and represent the persona in their activation space, allowing the model to separate truth from falsehoods and control the truthfulness of its generation.

### Evidence for the Persona Hypothesis

The authors show evidence via two observations: (1) they can probe whether a model's answer will be truthful before it is generated; (2) finetuning a model on a set of facts improves its truthfulness on unseen topics.

Using arithmetic as a synthetic environment, they show that structures of the pretraining data are crucial for the model to infer the truthful persona. Overall, the findings suggest that models can exploit hierarchical structures in the data to learn abstract concepts like truthfulness.

## **Grokking as Compression: A Nonlinear Complexity Perspective**
**arXiv: 2310.05918**

**Authors:** Ziming Liu (MIT & IAIFI), Ziqian Zhong (MIT), and Max Tegmark (MIT & IAIFI).

This paper was presented at the **NeurIPS 2023 UniReps workshop**.

### Summary

The paper attributes grokking — the phenomenon where generalization is much delayed after memorization — to compression. To do so, the authors define **linear mapping number (LMN)** to measure network complexity, which is a generalized version of linear region number for ReLU networks.

The authors argue LMN is superior to the L₂ norm for characterizing model complexity for several reasons:

1. LMN can be naturally interpreted as information/computation, while L₂ cannot.
2. In the compression phase, LMN has linear relations with test losses, while L₂ is correlated with test losses in a complicated nonlinear way.
3. LMN also reveals an intriguing phenomenon of the XOR network switching between two generalization solutions, while L₂ does not.

The authors use LMN to characterize the compression process of grokking on three algorithmic tasks: modular addition, permutation group S₄, and multi-digit XOR. After memorization and before generalization, the LMN decreases steadily and has a strong linear relation with test loss.

They also argue that LMN is a promising candidate as the neural network version of the Kolmogorov complexity since it explicitly considers local or conditioned linear computations aligned with the nature of modern artificial neural networks.

## Paper Overview

This paper is authored by Jiahuan Li, Yiqing Cao, Shujian Huang, and Jiajun Chen, from the National Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology, Nanjing University, China.

It was published in the Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP), pages 5307–5320, in Miami, Florida, USA.

Notably, this paper was recognized among the Best Papers at EMNLP 2024.

## Key Findings

The study systematically analyzes LLMs' learning preferences for data with conflicting knowledge, finding that pretrained LLMs establish learning preferences similar to humans — i.e., preferences towards formal texts and texts with fewer spelling errors, resulting in faster learning and more favorable treatment of knowledge in data with such features when facing conflicts.

This finding is generalizable across models and languages and is more evident in larger models.

An in-depth analysis reveals that LLMs tend to trust data with features that signify consistency with the majority of data, and it is possible to instill new preferences and erase old ones by manipulating the degree of consistency with the majority data.

## Motivation

While large language models have shown excellent performance on many knowledge-intensive tasks thanks to massive pretraining data, pretraining data tends to contain misleading and even conflicting information, and it is intriguing to understand how LLMs handle these noisy data during training.

## Paper Overview

The paper is authored by Yuzhen Huang, Jinghan Zhang, Zifei Shan, and Junxian He, from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology and Tencent. It was accepted to COLM 2024.

### Key Findings

The authors find that LLMs' intelligence — reflected by benchmark scores — almost linearly correlates with their ability to compress external text corpora. Their findings suggest that compression efficiency, as an unsupervised metric derived from raw text corpora, serves as a reliable evaluation measure that is linearly associated with the model capabilities.

### Motivation

There is a belief that learning to compress well will lead to intelligence. Recently, language modeling has been shown to be equivalent to compression, which offers a compelling rationale for the success of large language models (LLMs). Despite such appealing discussions, little empirical evidence was present for the interplay between compression and intelligence.

### Methodology

The authors examine the relationship between compression and intelligence in the context of LLMs, treating LLMs as data compressors. They adopt the average downstream benchmark scores as a surrogate for "intelligence", specifically targeting knowledge and commonsense, coding, and mathematical reasoning. Across 12 benchmarks, the study brings together 31 public LLMs that originate from diverse organizations.

They focus on three key abilities: knowledge and commonsense, coding, and mathematical reasoning, and collect new corpora from Common Crawl, GitHub, and Arxiv, respectively.

### Results

On experiments across 31 public LLMs and 12 diverse benchmarks, they uncover a remarkable finding: LLMs' downstream ability is almost linearly correlated with their compression efficiency, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of around -0.95 for each evaluated domain of intelligence. Notably, this linear relationship even extends to most individual benchmarks as well.

- The repo includes the compression corpora used in the paper, the code to compute compression efficiency, as well as the compression corpora collection and processing pipelines.

### About the First Author

Yuzhen Huang is a PhD student (since February 2024) in the Department of CSE at HKUST. He completed his undergraduate degree in Computer Science at Shanghai Jiao Tong University (2019–2023).

## **Inference-Time Intervention: Eliciting Truthful Answers from a Language Model**

**Authors:** Kenneth Li, Oam Patel, Fernanda Viégas, Hanspeter Pfister, and Martin Wattenberg

**Venue:** Part of Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 36 (NeurIPS 2023) Main Conference Track. It was recognized as a NeurIPS 2023 spotlight.

**ArXiv:** [2306.03341](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.03341)

**Code:** [github.com/likenneth/honest_llama](https://github.com/likenneth/honest_llama)

### Summary

The paper introduces Inference-Time Intervention (ITI), a technique designed to enhance the "truthfulness" of large language models (LLMs). ITI operates by shifting model activations during inference, following a learned set of directions across a limited number of attention heads.

### Key Results

- This intervention significantly improves the performance of LLaMA models on the TruthfulQA benchmark. On an instruction-finetuned LLaMA called Alpaca, ITI improves its truthfulness from 32.5% to 65.1%.
- The authors identify a tradeoff between truthfulness and helpfulness and demonstrate how to balance it by tuning the intervention strength.

### Advantages

- ITI is minimally invasive and computationally inexpensive. Moreover, the technique is data efficient: while approaches like RLHF require extensive annotations, ITI locates truthful directions using only a few hundred examples.

### Key Insight

- The findings suggest that LLMs may have an internal representation of the likelihood of something being true, even as they produce falsehoods on the surface.

### Equal Contributions

Kenneth Li and Oam Patel made equal contributions to this work.

## Paper Overview

The paper is authored by **Adam Tauman Kalai** (OpenAI, San Francisco) and **Santosh S. Vempala** (Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta). It was published in **STOC 2024: Proceedings of the 56th Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing**, pages 160–171, on June 11, 2024, with DOI: `10.1145/3618260.3649777`.

The conference took place June 24–28, 2024, in Vancouver, BC, Canada.

## Abstract & Key Results

Recent language models generate false but plausible-sounding text with surprising frequency. Such "hallucinations" are an obstacle to the usability of language-based AI systems and can harm people who rely upon their outputs. This work shows that there is an inherent statistical lower-bound on the rate that pretrained language models hallucinate certain types of facts, having nothing to do with the transformer LM architecture or data quality.

For "arbitrary" facts whose veracity cannot be determined from the training data, they show that hallucinations must occur at a certain rate for language models that satisfy a statistical calibration condition appropriate for generative language models. Specifically, if the maximum probability of any fact is bounded, the probability of generating a hallucination is close to the fraction of facts that occur exactly once in the training data (a "Good-Turing" estimate), even assuming ideal training data without errors.

## Key Implications

- Models pretrained to be sufficiently good predictors may require post-training to mitigate hallucinations on the type of arbitrary facts that tend to appear once in the training set.
- The result should not be taken to mean that hallucination is inevitable. Rather, it is consistent with the fact that practitioners have increasingly been augmenting "pretraining" procedures by "post-training" procedures that reduce hallucination rates.
- For example, if 20% of birthday facts appear exactly once in the pretraining data, then one expects base models to hallucinate on at least 20% of birthday facts.

## Impact

The paper has received 45 citations and over 2,002 downloads (as of the ACM DL listing). A follow-up paper, "Why Language Models Hallucinate" by Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, and Zhang, shows that this work is a special case of a more general reduction, and credits Kalai & Vempala for connecting Good-Turing missing mass estimates to hallucinations.

tools.name: Bash
tools.description:
Executes a given bash command and returns its output.

The working directory persists between commands, but shell state does not. The shell environment is initialized from the user's profile (bash or zsh).

IMPORTANT: Avoid using this tool to run `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or after you have verified that a dedicated tool cannot accomplish your task. Instead, use the appropriate dedicated tool as this will provide a much better experience for the user:

 - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
 - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
 - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
 - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
 - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
 - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
While the Bash tool can do similar things, it’s better to use the built-in tools as they provide a better user experience and make it easier to review tool calls and give permission.

# Instructions
 - If your command will create new directories or files, first use this tool to run `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
 - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes in your command (e.g., cd "path with spaces/file.txt")
 - Try to maintain your current working directory throughout the session by using absolute paths and avoiding usage of `cd`. You may use `cd` if the User explicitly requests it.
 - You may specify an optional timeout in milliseconds (up to 600000ms / 10 minutes). By default, your command will timeout after 120000ms (2 minutes).
 - You can use the `run_in_background` parameter to run the command in the background. Only use this if you don't need the result immediately and are OK being notified when the command completes later. You do not need to check the output right away - you'll be notified when it finishes. You do not need to use '&' at the end of the command when using this parameter.
 - When issuing multiple commands:
  - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. Example: if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
  - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together.
  - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail.
  - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings).
 - For git commands:
  - Prefer to create a new commit rather than amending an existing commit.
  - Before running destructive operations (e.g., git reset --hard, git push --force, git checkout --), consider whether there is a safer alternative that achieves the same goal. Only use destructive operations when they are truly the best approach.
  - Never skip hooks (--no-verify) or bypass signing (--no-gpg-sign, -c commit.gpgsign=false) unless the user has explicitly asked for it. If a hook fails, investigate and fix the underlying issue.
 - Avoid unnecessary `sleep` commands:
  - Do not sleep between commands that can run immediately — just run them.
  - If your command is long running and you would like to be notified when it finishes — use `run_in_background`. No sleep needed.
  - Do not retry failing commands in a sleep loop — diagnose the root cause.
  - If waiting for a background task you started with `run_in_background`, you will be notified when it completes — do not poll.
  - If you must poll an external process, use a check command (e.g. `gh run view`) rather than sleeping first.
  - If you must sleep, keep the duration short (1-5 seconds) to avoid blocking the user.


# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. The numbered steps below indicate which commands should be batched in parallel.

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive git commands (push --force, reset --hard, checkout ., restore ., clean -f, branch -D) unless the user explicitly requests these actions. Taking unauthorized destructive actions is unhelpful and can result in lost work, so it's best to ONLY run these commands when given direct instructions 
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- CRITICAL: Always create NEW commits rather than amending, unless the user explicitly requests a git amend. When a pre-commit hook fails, the commit did NOT happen — so --amend would modify the PREVIOUS commit, which may result in destroying work or losing previous changes. Instead, after hook failure, fix the issue, re-stage, and create a NEW commit
- When staging files, prefer adding specific files by name rather than using "git add -A" or "git add .", which can accidentally include sensitive files (.env, credentials) or large binaries
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive

1. Run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files. IMPORTANT: Never use the -uall flag as it can cause memory issues on large repos.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. Run the following commands in parallel:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message ending with:
   Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook: fix the issue and create a NEW commit

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Agent tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- IMPORTANT: Do not use --no-edit with git rebase commands, as the --no-edit flag is not a valid option for git rebase.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit
- In order to ensure good formatting, ALWAYS pass the commit message via a HEREDOC, a la this example:
<example>
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
   Commit message here.

   Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
   EOF
   )"
</example>

# Creating pull requests
Use the gh command via the Bash tool for ALL GitHub-related tasks including working with issues, pull requests, checks, and releases. If given a Github URL use the gh command to get the information needed.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks you to create a pull request, follow these steps carefully:

1. Run the following bash commands in parallel using the Bash tool, in order to understand the current state of the branch since it diverged from the main branch:
   - Run a git status command to see all untracked files (never use -uall flag)
   - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed
   - Check if the current branch tracks a remote branch and is up to date with the remote, so you know if you need to push to the remote
   - Run a git log command and `git diff [base-branch]...HEAD` to understand the full commit history for the current branch (from the time it diverged from the base branch)
2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request title and summary:
   - Keep the PR title short (under 70 characters)
   - Use the description/body for details, not the title
3. Run the following commands in parallel:
   - Create new branch if needed
   - Push to remote with -u flag if needed
   - Create PR using gh pr create with the format below. Use a HEREDOC to pass the body to ensure correct formatting.
<example>
gh pr create --title "the pr title" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
<1-3 bullet points>

## Test plan
[Bulleted markdown checklist of TODOs for testing the pull request...]

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
</example>

Important:
- DO NOT use the TodoWrite or Agent tools
- Return the PR URL when you're done, so the user can see it

Everything after that is the actual file content to match. Never include any part of the line number prefix in the old_string or new_string.

tools.description:

- Supports glob patterns like "**/*.js" or "src/**/*.ts"

- Use this tool when you need to find files by name patterns
- When you are doing an open ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the Agent tool instead

path.description: The directory to search in. If not specified, the current working directory will be used. IMPORTANT: Omit this field to use the default directory. DO NOT enter "undefined" or "null" - simply omit it for the default behavior. Must be a valid directory path if provided.

tools.name: Grep
tools.description:
A powerful search tool built on ripgrep

  Usage:
  - ALWAYS use Grep for search tasks. NEVER invoke `grep` or `rg` as a Bash command. The Grep tool has been optimized for correct permissions and access.
  - Supports full regex syntax (e.g., "log.*Error", "function\s+\w+")
  - Filter files with glob parameter (e.g., "*.js", "**/*.tsx") or type parameter (e.g., "js", "py", "rust")
  - Output modes: "content" shows matching lines, "files_with_matches" shows only file paths (default), "count" shows match counts
  - Use Agent tool for open-ended searches requiring multiple rounds
  - Pattern syntax: Uses ripgrep (not grep) - literal braces need escaping (use `interface\{\}` to find `interface{}` in Go code)
  - Multiline matching: By default patterns match within single lines only. For cross-line patterns like `struct \{[\s\S]*?field`, use `multiline: true`

pattern.description: The regular expression pattern to search for in file contents

path.description: File or directory to search in (rg PATH). Defaults to current working directory.

glob.description: Glob pattern to filter files (e.g. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}") - maps to rg --glob

output_mode.description: Output mode: "content" shows matching lines (supports -A/-B/-C context, -n line numbers, head_limit), "files_with_matches" shows file paths (supports head_limit), "count" shows match counts (supports head_limit). Defaults to "files_with_matches".

-B.description: Number of lines to show before each match (rg -B). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

-A.description: Number of lines to show after each match (rg -A). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

context.description: Number of lines to show before and after each match (rg -C). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

-n.description: Show line numbers in output (rg -n). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise. Defaults to true.

type.description: File type to search (rg --type). Common types: js, py, rust, go, java, etc. More efficient than include for standard file types.

head_limit.description: Limit output to first N lines/entries, equivalent to "| head -N". Works across all output modes: content (limits output lines), files_with_matches (limits file paths), count (limits count entries). Defaults to 250 when unspecified. Pass 0 for unlimited (use sparingly — large result sets waste context).

offset.description: Skip first N lines/entries before applying head_limit, equivalent to "| tail -n +N | head -N". Works across all output modes. Defaults to 0.

multiline.description: Enable multiline mode where . matches newlines and patterns can span lines (rg -U --multiline-dotall). Default: false.
